[[File:Blacksmith Munechika, helped by a fox spirit, forging the blade Ko-Gitsune Maru, by Ogata Gekkō.jpg|thumb|200px|Illustration: *Inari Mountain Swordsmith* by [[Ogata Gekkō]], based on the Noh play *[[Ko-Kaji]]*.]]
'''Ko-Gitsune Maru''' (小狐丸) is a legendary [[Japanese sword]] (*[[tachi]]*) said to have been forged during the [[Heian period]]. While multiple swords share this name, this article focuses on the blade attributed to the famed swordsmith [[Sanjō Munechika]].
== Overview == The sword is considered a masterpiece passed down within the [[Fujiwara clan]], with strong support for the theory that it was created by [[Sanjō Munechika]]<ref>{{Citation|和書|last=福永|first=酔剣|title=日本刀大百科事典|volume=2|publisher=[[雄山閣出版]]|date=1993-11-20|isbn=4639012020|ncid=BN10133913|page=234}}</ref>. By the late Heian period, the blade was in the possession of the [[Kujō family]], later passing to the [[Takatsukasa family]] during the late [[Kamakura period]], before eventually being lost{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}.
At [[Ishikiri Shrine]] in [[Higashiōsaka, Osaka]], a sword said to be the work of Munechika is enshrined. It is displayed to the public alongside the [[Ishikiri Maru]], a sword attributed to the [[Sanjō school]], during the shrine’s spring festival in April and autumn festival in October<ref name="刀剣ワールド">[https://www.touken-world.jp/religious-building/12991/ 石切劔箭神社（大阪府東大阪市） - 刀剣ワールド]2019年11月17日閲覧</ref>. This version of Ko-Gitsune Maru, at approximately 53.8 cm long, is classified as a [[wakizashi]] and is believed to differ from the tachi passed down by the Fujiwara clan<ref name="刀剣ワールド" />. However, its shortened tang and preserved signature suggest it was originally longer<ref name="刀剣ワールド" />.
Another sword named Ko-Gitsune Maru is housed at [[Isonokami Shrine]]. As of September 2018, it has been designated a Tangible Cultural Property of [[Nara Prefecture]]<ref name="石上神宮">[http://www.isonokami.jp/about/c4.html ご由緒【宝物】 - 石上神宮]2019年11月17日閲覧</ref><ref name="奈良県">[http://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/143530/kenshiitiran.pdf 奈良県指定文化財一覧 - 奈良県（PDF版）]2019年11月17日閲覧</ref>. According to its legend, it was stolen during the late Edo period from the [[Emperor Suinin Mausoleum]] by grave robbers and sold to [[Takatsukasa Masamichi]], who, upon discovering its origin, donated it to the shrine{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}. The blade bears the signature of “Yoshinori,” attributed to the [[Ko-Bizen school]]{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}.

While some characters, such as Tachibana Michinari, lack historical evidence, other details suggest connections to Emperor [[Ichijō]]{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}.
==== Synopsis ==== The Emperor [[Ichijō]] orders Tachibana Michinari to commission a protective sword. Michinari turns to Sanjō Munechika, renowned for his craft{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}. Struggling to forge a satisfactory blade, Munechika prays to his tutelary deity, [[Inari]], and encounters a mysterious child who promises help. The child later reveals themselves as the deity Inari, assisting Munechika in crafting a divine blade. The finished sword, of exceptional quality, is named Ko-Gitsune Maru{{sfn|小和田|2015|p=31}}.

While some characters, such as Tachibana Michinari, lack historical evidence, other details suggest connections to Emperor [[Ichijō]]{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}.
==== Synopsis ==== The Emperor [[Ichijō]] orders Tachibana Michinari to commission a protective sword. Michinari turns to Sanjō Munechika, renowned for his craft{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}. Struggling to forge a satisfactory blade, Munechika prays to his tutelary deity, [[Inari]], and encounters a mysterious child who promises help. The child later reveals themselves as the deity Inari, assisting Munechika in crafting a divine blade. The finished sword, of exceptional quality, is named Ko-Gitsune Maru{{sfn|小和田|2015|p=31}}.

== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == *[[List of Japanese Swords]] *[[Aitsuchi Inari Shrine]]

revision.text:
[[File:Blacksmith Munechika, helped by a fox spirit, forging the blade Ko-Gitsune Maru, by Ogata Gekkō.jpg|thumb|200px|Illustration: *Inari Mountain Swordsmith* by [[Ogata Gekkō]], based on the Noh play *[[Ko-Kaji]]*.]]
'''Ko-Gitsune Maru''' (小狐丸) is a legendary [[Japanese sword]] (*[[tachi]]*) said to have been forged during the [[Heian period]]. While multiple swords share this name, this article focuses on the blade attributed to the famed swordsmith [[Sanjō Munechika]].
== Overview == The sword is considered a masterpiece passed down within the [[Fujiwara clan]], with strong support for the theory that it was created by [[Sanjō Munechika]]<ref>{{Citation|和書|last=福永|first=酔剣|title=日本刀大百科事典|volume=2|publisher=[[雄山閣出版]]|date=1993-11-20|isbn=4639012020|ncid=BN10133913|page=234}}</ref>. By the late Heian period, the blade was in the possession of the [[Kujō family]], later passing to the [[Takatsukasa family]] during the late [[Kamakura period]], before eventually being lost{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}.
At [[Ishikiri Shrine]] in [[Higashiōsaka, Osaka]], a sword said to be the work of Munechika is enshrined. It is displayed to the public alongside the [[Ishikiri Maru]], a sword attributed to the [[Sanjō school]], during the shrine’s spring festival in April and autumn festival in October<ref name="刀剣ワールド">[https://www.touken-world.jp/religious-building/12991/ 石切劔箭神社（大阪府東大阪市） - 刀剣ワールド]2019th year 11月17日閲覧</ref>. This version of Ko-Gitsune Maru, at approximately 53.8 cm long, is classified as a [[wakizashi]] and is believed to differ from the tachi passed down by the Fujiwara clan<ref name="刀剣ワールド" />. However, its shortened tang and preserved signature suggest it was originally longer<ref name="刀剣ワールド" />.
Another sword named Ko-Gitsune Maru is housed at [[Isonokami Shrine]]. As of September 2018, it has been designated a Tangible Cultural Property of [[Nara Prefecture]]<ref name="石上神宮">[http://www.isonokami.jp/about/c4.html ご由緒【宝物】 - 石上神宮]2019th year 11月17日閲覧</ref><ref name="奈良県">[http://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/143530/kenshiitiran.pdf 奈良県指定文化財一覧 - 奈良県（PDF版）]2019th year 11月17日閲覧</ref>. According to its legend, it was stolen during the late Edo period from the [[Emperor Suinin Mausoleum]] by grave robbers and sold to [[Takatsukasa Masamichi]], who, upon discovering its origin, donated it to the shrine{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}. The blade bears the signature of “Yoshinori,” attributed to the [[Ko-Bizen school]]{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}.
== Related Works == === Noh Play "Ko-Kaji" === {{能楽作品 |演目名=小鍛冶 |作者（年代）=不詳（室町時代） |形式= |能柄=五番目もの |現行上演流派= |異称= |シテ=稲荷明神 |登場人物=三条宗近、橘道成 |季節= |場所= |本説= |よみかな=こかし }} The play *[[Ko-Kaji]]* (*The Little Swordsmith*), written during the [[Muromachi period]], recounts the tale of Sanjō Munechika and the divine assistance he receives from the god [[Inari]]. While some characters, such as Tachibana Michinari, lack historical evidence, other details suggest connections to Emperor [[Ichijō]]{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}.
==== Synopsis ==== The Emperor [[Ichijō]] orders Tachibana Michinari to commission a protective sword. Michinari turns to Sanjō Munechika, renowned for his craft{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}. Struggling to forge a satisfactory blade, Munechika prays to his tutelary deity, [[Inari]], and encounters a mysterious child who promises help. The child later reveals themselves as the deity Inari, assisting Munechika in crafting a divine blade. The finished sword, of exceptional quality, is named Ko-Gitsune Maru{{sfn|小和田|2015|p=31}}.
== Notes == {{Reflist|group="注釈"}}
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == *[[List of Japanese Swords]] *[[Aitsuchi Inari Shrine]]
{{Weapon-stub}} {{citation needed|こきつねまる}}

As of September 2018, it has been designated a Tangible Cultural Property of [[Nara Prefecture]]<ref name="石上神宮">[http://www.isonokami.jp/about/c4.html ご由緒【宝物】 - 石上神宮]2019th year 11月17日閲覧</ref><ref name="奈良県">[http://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/143530/kenshiitiran.pdf 奈良県指定文化財一覧 - 奈良県（PDF版）]2019th year 11月17日閲覧</ref>. == Related Works == === Noh Play "Ko-Kaji" === {{能楽作品 |演目名=小鍛冶 |作者（年代）=不詳（室町時代） |形式= |能柄=五番目もの |現行上演流派= |異称= |シテ=稲荷明神 |登場人物=三条宗近、橘道成 |季節= |場所= |本説= |よみかな=こかし }} The play *[[Ko-Kaji]]* (*The Little Swordsmith*), written during the [[Muromachi period]], recounts the tale of Sanjō Munechika and the divine assistance he receives from the god [[Inari]]. While some characters, such as Tachibana Michinari, lack historical evidence, other details suggest connections to Emperor [[Ichijō]]{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}.
==== Synopsis ==== The Emperor [[Ichijō]] orders Tachibana Michinari to commission a protective sword. Michinari turns to Sanjō Munechika, renowned for his craft{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}. Struggling to forge a satisfactory blade, Munechika prays to his tutelary deity, [[Inari]], and encounters a mysterious child who promises help. The child later reveals themselves as the deity Inari, assisting Munechika in crafting a divine blade. The finished sword, of exceptional quality, is named Ko-Gitsune Maru{{sfn|小和田|2015|p=31}}.
== Notes == {{Reflist|group="注釈"}}
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == *[[List of Japanese Swords]] *[[Aitsuchi Inari Shrine]]

As of September 2018, it has been designated a Tangible Cultural Property of [[Nara Prefecture]]<ref name="石上神宮">[http://www.isonokami.jp/about/c4.html ご由緒【宝物】 - 石上神宮]2019th year 11月17日閲覧</ref><ref name="奈良県">[http://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/143530/kenshiitiran.pdf 奈良県指定文化財一覧 - 奈良県（PDF版）]2019th year 11月17日閲覧</ref>. According to its legend, it was stolen during the late Edo period from the [[Emperor Suinin Mausoleum]] by grave robbers and sold to [[Takatsukasa Masamichi]], who, upon discovering its origin, donated it to the shrine{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}. The blade bears the signature of “Yoshinori,” attributed to the [[Ko-Bizen school]]{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}.
== Related Works == === Noh Play "Ko-Kaji" === {{能楽作品 |演目名=小鍛冶 |作者（年代）=不詳（室町時代） |形式= |能柄=五番目もの |現行上演流派= |異称= |シテ=稲荷明神 |登場人物=三条宗近、橘道成 |季節= |場所= |本説= |よみかな=こかし }} The play *[[Ko-Kaji]]* (*The Little Swordsmith*), written during the [[Muromachi period]], recounts the tale of Sanjō Munechika and the divine assistance he receives from the god [[Inari]]. While some characters, such as Tachibana Michinari, lack historical evidence, other details suggest connections to Emperor [[Ichijō]]{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}.
==== Synopsis ==== The Emperor [[Ichijō]] orders Tachibana Michinari to commission a protective sword. Michinari turns to Sanjō Munechika, renowned for his craft{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}. Struggling to forge a satisfactory blade, Munechika prays to his tutelary deity, [[Inari]], and encounters a mysterious child who promises help. The child later reveals themselves as the deity Inari, assisting Munechika in crafting a divine blade. The finished sword, of exceptional quality, is named Ko-Gitsune Maru{{sfn|小和田|2015|p=31}}.
== Notes == {{Reflist|group="注釈"}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == *[[List of Japanese Swords]] *[[Aitsuchi Inari Shrine]]
{{Weapon-stub}} {{citation needed|こきつねまる}}
<!--Kokitsunemaru-->
{{Isonokami Faith}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Renowned Japanese Swords]] [[Category:Japanese swords]] [[Category:Kokitsunemaru]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] }} [[ja:小狐丸]] [[sn:Kokitsunemaru]] [[sn:okitsunemaru]]

[[File:Blacksmith Munechika, helped by a fox spirit, forging the blade Ko-Gitsune Maru, by Ogata Gekkō.jpg|thumb|200px|Illustration: *Inari Mountain Swordsmith* by [[Ogata Gekkō]], based on the Noh play *[[Ko-Kaji]]*.]]
'''Ko-Gitsune Maru''' (小狐丸) is a legendary [[Japanese sword]] (*[[tachi]]*) said to have been forged during the [[Heian period]]. While multiple swords share this name, this article focuses on the blade attributed to the famed swordsmith [[Sanjō Munechika]].
== Overview == The sword is considered a masterpiece passed down within the [[Fujiwara clan]], with strong support for the theory that it was created by [[Sanjō Munechika]]<ref>{{Citation|和書|last=福永|first=酔剣|title=日本刀大百科事典|volume=2|publisher=[[雄山閣出版]]|date=1993-11-20|isbn=4639012020|ncid=BN10133913|page=234}}</ref>. By the late Heian period, the blade was in the possession of the [[Kujō family]], later passing to the [[Takatsukasa family]] during the late [[Kamakura period]], before eventually being lost{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}.
At [[Ishikiri Shrine]] in [[Higashiōsaka, Osaka]], a sword said to be the work of Munechika is enshrined. It is displayed to the public alongside the [[Ishikiri Maru]], a sword attributed to the [[Sanjō school]], during the shrine’s spring festival in April and autumn festival in October<ref name="刀剣ワールド">[https://www.touken-world.jp/religious-building/12991/ 石切劔箭神社（大阪府東大阪市） - 刀剣ワールド]2019th year 11月17日閲覧</ref>. This version of Ko-Gitsune Maru, at approximately 53.8 cm long, is classified as a [[wakizashi]] and is believed to differ from the tachi passed down by the Fujiwara clan<ref name="刀剣ワールド" />. However, its shortened tang and preserved signature suggest it was originally longer<ref name="刀剣ワールド" />.
Another sword named Ko-Gitsune Maru is housed at [[Isonokami Shrine]]. As of September 2018, it has been designated a Tangible Cultural Property of [[Nara Prefecture]]<ref name="石上神宮">[http://www.isonokami.jp/about/c4.html ご由緒【宝物】 - 石上神宮]2019th year 11月17日閲覧</ref><ref name="奈良県">[http://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/143530/kenshiitiran.pdf 奈良県指定文化財一覧 - 奈良県（PDF版）]2019th year 11月17日閲覧</ref>. According to its legend, it was stolen during the late Edo period from the [[Emperor Suinin Mausoleum]] by grave robbers and sold to [[Takatsukasa Masamichi]], who, upon discovering its origin, donated it to the shrine{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}. The blade bears the signature of “Yoshinori,” attributed to the [[Ko-Bizen school]]{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}.
== Related Works == === Noh Play "Ko-Kaji" === {{能楽作品 |演目名=小鍛冶 |作者（年代）=不詳（室町時代） |形式= |能柄=五番目もの |現行上演流派= |異称= |シテ=稲荷明神 |登場人物=三条宗近、橘道成 |季節= |場所= |本説= |よみかな=こかし }} The play *[[Ko-Kaji]]* (*The Little Swordsmith*), written during the [[Muromachi period]], recounts the tale of Sanjō Munechika and the divine assistance he receives from the god [[Inari]]. While some characters, such as Tachibana Michinari, lack historical evidence, other details suggest connections to Emperor [[Ichijō]]{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}.
==== Synopsis ==== The Emperor [[Ichijō]] orders Tachibana Michinari to commission a protective sword. Michinari turns to Sanjō Munechika, renowned for his craft{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}. Struggling to forge a satisfactory blade, Munechika prays to his tutelary deity, [[Inari]], and encounters a mysterious child who promises help. The child later reveals themselves as the deity Inari, assisting Munechika in crafting a divine blade. The finished sword, of exceptional quality, is named Ko-Gitsune Maru{{sfn|小和田|2015|p=31}}.
== Notes == {{Reflist|group="注釈"}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == *[[List of Japanese Swords]] *[[Aitsuchi Inari Shrine]]

revision.text:
[[File:Blacksmith Munechika, helped by a fox spirit, forging the blade Ko-Gitsune Maru, by Ogata Gekkō.jpg|thumb|200px|『月耕随筆 稲荷山小鍛治』[[尾形月耕]]。[[謡曲]]の「[[小鍛冶]]」を題材としている]] '''小狐丸'''（こぎつねまる）は、[[平安時代]]に作られたとされる[[日本刀]]（[[太刀]]）である。同名複数の刀剣が存在するが、本項では[[三条宗近]]作と言い伝えられている日本刀を中心に説明する。
== 概要 == 藤原氏に伝来していた名刀とされており、[[三条宗近]]による作品とする説が濃厚とされる<ref>{{Citation|和書|last=福永|first=酔剣|title=日本刀大百科事典|volume=2|publisher=[[雄山閣出版]]|date=1993-11-20|isbn=4639012020|ncid=BN10133913|page=234}}</ref>。この小狐丸は平安時代末期には[[九条家]]にあり、鎌倉時代後期になると[[鷹司家]]にあったとされているが後に逸失している{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}。
[[大阪府]][[東大阪市]]の[[石切剣箭神社]]では、宗近作と言われる小狐丸が所蔵されており、4月の春大祭と10月の秋大祭の時に同神社に収蔵されている[[三条 (刀工)|三条派]]有成作の[[石切丸]]と共に一般公開される<ref name="刀剣ワールド">[https://www.touken-world.jp/religious-building/12991/ 石切劔箭神社（大阪府東大阪市） - 刀剣ワールド]2019年11月17日 閲覧</ref>。石切剣箭神社収蔵の小狐丸は、刃長が約53.8センチメートルと[[脇差]]サイズであることから、上記の藤原氏伝来の太刀とは別物ともいわれる<ref name="刀剣ワールド" />。しかし、折り返し銘が施されている{{refnest|group=注釈|「折り返し銘」とは、磨上げなどで茎が短くなり、銘がなくなってしまう場合に、反対側に折り曲げて銘を残すことを指す<ref name="刀剣ワールド" />。}}ことから、本来は現存の物よりも長かったと考えられている<ref name="刀剣ワールド" />。
また、[[石上神宮]]にも小狐丸と名付けられた太刀があり、2018年9月時点で奈良県の有形文化財に指定されている<ref name="石上神宮">[http://www.isonokami.jp/about/c4.html ご由緒【宝物】 - 石上神宮]2019年11月17日 閲覧</ref><ref name="奈良県">[http://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/143530/kenshiitiran.pdf 奈良県指定文化財一覧 - 奈良県（PDF版）]2019年11月17日 閲覧</ref>。この刀の伝来は、江戸時代末期に[[垂仁天皇陵]]を盗掘した盗人が小狐丸を盗み出し、その刀を[[鷹司政通]]に売り込んだ{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}。政通は不審に思って調査したところ刀は盗品であることが分かり、盗人は[[磔刑]]に処され刀は政通によって石上神宮に奉納されたことによる{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}。ただし、銘には「義憲作」と切られていることから[[古備前派]]の義憲によって作られたものとされている{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}。
== 関連作品 == === 謡曲『小鍛冶』 === {{能楽作品 |演目名=小鍛冶 |作者（年代）=不詳（室町時代） |形式= |能柄=五番目もの |現行上演流派= |異称= |シテ=稲荷明神 |登場人物=三条宗近、橘道成 |季節= |場所= |本説= |よみかな=こかし }} 『[[小鍛冶]]（こかじ）』は、室町時代に成立した[[謡曲]]。登場人物の橘道成という人物が橘氏系図に現れる人物ではないため史実かは疑わしいとされる{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}。だが、小和田泰経は、道長は一条天皇の岳父である関係から、一条天皇が逝去した際に道長へ下賜された可能性はあると指摘している{{sfn|小和田|2015|p=31}}。
==== 登場人物 ==== *前シテ:童子 *後シテ:稲荷明神 *ワキ:三条宗近 *ワキツレ:橘道成
==== あらすじ ==== 時の帝、[[一条天皇]]から守り刀を作るよう命ぜられた橘道成は、京の名工として知られる三条宗近に作刀を依頼した{{refnest|group=注釈|宗近は元々刀工ではなく、従四位下[[橘仲道]]の次男であり、従六位上信濃大掾に任ぜられた官人である傍ら、余暇に作刀を行っていたとする伝承がある<ref>{{Citation|和書|last=常石|first=英明|title=日本刀の歴史 古刀編|volume=|publisher=[[金園社]]|date=2016-05-10|isbn=9784321346528|ncid=BB21381049|page=38}}</ref>。}}{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}{{sfn|小和田|2015|p=31}}。しかし、宗近は満足のいく刀を打てず困り果て、[[氏神]]である[[稲荷神|稲荷明神]]に詣でて祈願しようとした道中に童子と遭遇し、童子は「安心してください。私が相槌を打ちましょう。」と言うと消え去ってしまった{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}{{sfn|小和田|2015|p=31}}。宗近は不思議に思いながらも作刀の準備を始めると童子に化けていた稲荷明神が現れて、宗近と共に太刀を作った{{sfn|小和田|2015|p=31}}。完成した太刀を見ると素晴らしい出来合いであったため、宗近は「小狐丸」と名付けたとされる{{sfn|小和田|2015|p=31}}。 {{-}} == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}}
=== 注釈 === {{Reflist|group="注釈"}}
=== 出典 === {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == {{参照方法|date=2019年11月}} *{{Citation|和書|last=小和田|first=泰経|title=刀剣目録|volume=|publisher=[[新紀元社]]|date=2015-06-12|isbn=4775313401|ncid=BB19726465}} *{{Citation|和書|last=かみゆ歴史編集部|first=編集|title=物語で読む日本の刀剣150|volume=|publisher=[[イースト・プレス]]|date=2015-05-20|isbn=9784781680026|ncid=BB18681317}} *赤井達郎『「寺と社」-古寺社への道-』講談社 1984年 *邦光史郎『「伝説とその舞台」-幽玄と不思議の世界-』講談社 1984年
== 関連項目 == *[[日本刀一覧]] *[[合槌稲荷神社]]
{{Weapon-stub}} {{デフォルトソート:こきつねまる}} [[Category:著名な日本刀]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:小狐丸]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11462866]] Wikidata name: kogitsunemaru [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11462866]] Wikidata name: kogitsunemaru [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Blacksmith Munechika, helped by a fox spirit, forging the blade Ko-Gitsune Maru, by Ogata Gekkō.jpg|thumb|200px|Illustration: *Inari Mountain Swordsmith* by [[:en:Ogata Gekkō|Ogata Gekkō]], based on the Noh play *[[Ko-Kaji]]*.]]
'''Ko-Gitsune Maru''' (小狐丸) is a legendary [[Japanese sword]] (*[[tachi]]*) said to have been forged during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. While multiple swords share this name, this article focuses on the blade attributed to the famed swordsmith [[Sanjō Munechika]].
== Overview == The sword is considered a masterpiece passed down within the [[Fujiwara clan]], with strong support for the theory that it was created by [[Sanjō Munechika]]<ref>{{Citation|和書|last=福永|first=酔剣|title=日本刀大百科事典|volume=2|publisher=[[雄山閣出版]]|date=1993-11-20|isbn=4639012020|ncid=BN10133913|page=234}}</ref>. By the late Heian period, the blade was in the possession of the [[:en:Kujō family|Kujō family]], later passing to the [[:en:Takatsukasa family|Takatsukasa family]] during the late [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], before eventually being lost{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}.
At [[Ishikiri Shrine]] in [[:en:Higashiōsaka, Osaka|Higashiōsaka, Osaka]], a sword said to be the work of Munechika is enshrined. It is displayed to the public alongside the [[Ishikiri Maru]], a sword attributed to the [[Sanjō school]], during the shrine’s spring festival in April and autumn festival in October<ref name="刀剣ワールド">[https://www.touken-world.jp/religious-building/12991/ 石切劔箭神社（大阪府東大阪市） - 刀剣ワールド]2019th year 11月17日閲覧</ref>. This version of Ko-Gitsune Maru, at approximately 53.8 cm long, is classified as a [[wakizashi]] and is believed to differ from the tachi passed down by the Fujiwara clan<ref name="刀剣ワールド" />. However, its shortened tang and preserved signature suggest it was originally longer<ref name="刀剣ワールド" />.
Another sword named Ko-Gitsune Maru is housed at [[Isonokami Jingu|Isonokami Shrine]]. As of September 2018, it has been designated a Tangible Cultural Property of [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]<ref name="石上神宮">[http://www.isonokami.jp/about/c4.html ご由緒【宝物】 - 石上神宮]2019th year 11月17日閲覧</ref><ref name="奈良県">[http://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/143530/kenshiitiran.pdf 奈良県指定文化財一覧 - 奈良県（PDF版）]2019th year 11月17日閲覧</ref>. According to its legend, it was stolen during the late Edo period from the [[Emperor Suinin Mausoleum]] by grave robbers and sold to [[:en:Takatsukasa Masamichi|Takatsukasa Masamichi]], who, upon discovering its origin, donated it to the shrine{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}. The blade bears the signature of “Yoshinori,” attributed to the [[Ko-Bizen school]]{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}.
== Related Works == === Noh Play "Ko-Kaji" === {{能楽作品 |演目名=小鍛冶 |作者（年代）=不詳（室町時代） |形式= |能柄=五番目もの |現行上演流派= |異称= |シテ=稲荷明神 |登場人物=三条宗近、橘道成 |季節= |場所= |本説= |よみかな=こかし }} The play *[[Ko-Kaji]]* (*The Little Swordsmith*), written during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], recounts the tale of Sanjō Munechika and the divine assistance he receives from the god [[:en:Inari|Inari]]. While some characters, such as Tachibana Michinari, lack historical evidence, other details suggest connections to Emperor [[:en:Ichijō|Ichijō]]{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}.
==== Synopsis ==== The Emperor [[:en:Ichijō|Ichijō]] orders Tachibana Michinari to commission a protective sword. Michinari turns to Sanjō Munechika, renowned for his craft{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}. Struggling to forge a satisfactory blade, Munechika prays to his tutelary deity, [[:en:Inari|Inari]], and encounters a mysterious child who promises help. The child later reveals themselves as the deity Inari, assisting Munechika in crafting a divine blade. The finished sword, of exceptional quality, is named Ko-Gitsune Maru{{sfn|小和田|2015|p=31}}.
== Notes == {{Reflist|group="Note"}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == *[[List of Japanese Swords]] *[[Aitsuchi Inari Shrine]]

As of September 2018, it has been designated a Tangible Cultural Property of [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]<ref name="石上神宮">[http://www.isonokami.jp/about/c4.html ご由緒【宝物】 - 石上神宮]2019th year 11月17日閲覧</ref><ref name="奈良県">[http://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/143530/kenshiitiran.pdf 奈良県指定文化財一覧 - 奈良県（PDF版）]2019th year 11月17日閲覧</ref>. According to its legend, it was stolen during the late Edo period from the [[Emperor Suinin Mausoleum]] by grave robbers and sold to [[:en:Takatsukasa Masamichi|Takatsukasa Masamichi]], who, upon discovering its origin, donated it to the shrine{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}. The blade bears the signature of “Yoshinori,” attributed to the [[Ko-Bizen school]]{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}.
== Related Works == === Noh Play "Ko-Kaji" === {{能楽作品 |演目名=小鍛冶 |作者（年代）=不詳（室町時代） |形式= |能柄=五番目もの |現行上演流派= |異称= |シテ=稲荷明神 |登場人物=三条宗近、橘道成 |季節= |場所= |本説= |よみかな=こかし }} The play *[[Ko-Kaji]]* (*The Little Swordsmith*), written during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], recounts the tale of Sanjō Munechika and the divine assistance he receives from the god [[:en:Inari|Inari]]. While some characters, such as Tachibana Michinari, lack historical evidence, other details suggest connections to Emperor [[:en:Ichijō|Ichijō]]{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}.
==== Synopsis ==== The Emperor [[:en:Ichijō|Ichijō]] orders Tachibana Michinari to commission a protective sword. Michinari turns to Sanjō Munechika, renowned for his craft{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}. Struggling to forge a satisfactory blade, Munechika prays to his tutelary deity, [[:en:Inari|Inari]], and encounters a mysterious child who promises help. The child later reveals themselves as the deity Inari, assisting Munechika in crafting a divine blade. The finished sword, of exceptional quality, is named Ko-Gitsune Maru{{sfn|小和田|2015|p=31}}.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:小狐丸]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11462866]] Wikidata name: kogitsunemaru [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11462866]] Wikidata name: kogitsunemaru [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Blacksmith Munechika, helped by a fox spirit, forging the blade Ko-Gitsune Maru, by Ogata Gekkō.jpg|thumb|200px|Illustration: *Inari Mountain Swordsmith* by [[:en:Ogata Gekkō|Ogata Gekkō]], based on the Noh play *[[Ko-Kaji]]*.]]
'''Ko-Gitsune Maru''' (小狐丸) is a legendary [[Japanese sword]] (*[[tachi]]*) said to have been forged during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. While multiple swords share this name, this article focuses on the blade attributed to the famed swordsmith [[Sanjō Munechika]].
== Overview == The sword is considered a masterpiece passed down within the [[Fujiwara clan]], with strong support for the theory that it was created by [[Sanjō Munechika]]<ref>{{Citation|和書|last=福永|first=酔剣|title=日本刀大百科事典|volume=2|publisher=[[雄山閣出版]]|date=1993-11-20|isbn=4639012020|ncid=BN10133913|page=234}}</ref>. By the late Heian period, the blade was in the possession of the [[:en:Kujō family|Kujō family]], later passing to the [[:en:Takatsukasa family|Takatsukasa family]] during the late [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], before eventually being lost{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}.
At [[Ishikiri Shrine]] in [[:en:Higashiōsaka, Osaka|Higashiōsaka, Osaka]], a sword said to be the work of Munechika is enshrined. It is displayed to the public alongside the [[Ishikiri Maru]], a sword attributed to the [[Sanjō school]], during the shrine’s spring festival in April and autumn festival in October<ref name="刀剣ワールド">[https://www.touken-world.jp/religious-building/12991/ 石切劔箭神社（大阪府東大阪市） - 刀剣ワールド]2019th year 11月17日閲覧</ref>. This version of Ko-Gitsune Maru, at approximately 53.8 cm long, is classified as a [[wakizashi]] and is believed to differ from the tachi passed down by the Fujiwara clan<ref name="刀剣ワールド" />. However, its shortened tang and preserved signature suggest it was originally longer<ref name="刀剣ワールド" />.
Another sword named Ko-Gitsune Maru is housed at [[Isonokami Jingu|Isonokami Shrine]]. As of September 2018, it has been designated a Tangible Cultural Property of [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]<ref name="石上神宮">[http://www.isonokami.jp/about/c4.html ご由緒【宝物】 - 石上神宮]2019th year 11月17日閲覧</ref><ref name="奈良県">[http://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/143530/kenshiitiran.pdf 奈良県指定文化財一覧 - 奈良県（PDF版）]2019th year 11月17日閲覧</ref>. According to its legend, it was stolen during the late Edo period from the [[Emperor Suinin Mausoleum]] by grave robbers and sold to [[:en:Takatsukasa Masamichi|Takatsukasa Masamichi]], who, upon discovering its origin, donated it to the shrine{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}. The blade bears the signature of “Yoshinori,” attributed to the [[Ko-Bizen school]]{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}.
== Related Works == === Noh Play "Ko-Kaji" === {{能楽作品 |演目名=小鍛冶 |作者（年代）=不詳（室町時代） |形式= |能柄=五番目もの |現行上演流派= |異称= |シテ=稲荷明神 |登場人物=三条宗近、橘道成 |季節= |場所= |本説= |よみかな=こかし }} The play *[[Ko-Kaji]]* (*The Little Swordsmith*), written during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], recounts the tale of Sanjō Munechika and the divine assistance he receives from the god [[:en:Inari|Inari]]. While some characters, such as Tachibana Michinari, lack historical evidence, other details suggest connections to Emperor [[:en:Ichijō|Ichijō]]{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}.
==== Synopsis ==== The Emperor [[:en:Ichijō|Ichijō]] orders Tachibana Michinari to commission a protective sword. Michinari turns to Sanjō Munechika, renowned for his craft{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}. Struggling to forge a satisfactory blade, Munechika prays to his tutelary deity, [[:en:Inari|Inari]], and encounters a mysterious child who promises help. The child later reveals themselves as the deity Inari, assisting Munechika in crafting a divine blade. The finished sword, of exceptional quality, is named Ko-Gitsune Maru{{sfn|小和田|2015|p=31}}.

Michinari turns to Sanjō Munechika, renowned for his craft{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}. Struggling to forge a satisfactory blade, Munechika prays to his tutelary deity, [[:en:Inari|Inari]], and encounters a mysterious child who promises help. The child later reveals themselves as the deity Inari, assisting Munechika in crafting a divine blade. The finished sword, of exceptional quality, is named Ko-Gitsune Maru{{sfn|小和田|2015|p=31}}.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:小狐丸]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:小狐丸]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11462866]] Wikidata name: kogitsunemaru [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11462866]] Wikidata name: kogitsunemaru [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Blacksmith Munechika, helped by a fox spirit, forging the blade Ko-Gitsune Maru, by Ogata Gekkō.jpg|thumb|200px|Illustration: *Inari Mountain Swordsmith* by [[:en:Ogata Gekkō|Ogata Gekkō]], based on the Noh play *[[Ko-Kaji]]*.]]
'''Ko-Gitsune Maru''' (小狐丸) is a legendary [[Japanese sword]] (*[[tachi]]*) said to have been forged during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. While multiple swords share this name, this article focuses on the blade attributed to the famed swordsmith [[Sanjō Munechika]].
== Overview == The sword is considered a masterpiece passed down within the [[Fujiwara clan]], with strong support for the theory that it was created by [[Sanjō Munechika]]<ref>{{Citation|和書|last=福永|first=酔剣|title=日本刀大百科事典|volume=2|publisher=[[雄山閣出版]]|date=1993-11-20|isbn=4639012020|ncid=BN10133913|page=234}}</ref>. By the late Heian period, the blade was in the possession of the [[:en:Kujō family|Kujō family]], later passing to the [[:en:Takatsukasa family|Takatsukasa family]] during the late [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], before eventually being lost{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}.
At [[Ishikiri Shrine]] in [[:en:Higashiōsaka, Osaka|Higashiōsaka, Osaka]], a sword said to be the work of Munechika is enshrined. It is displayed to the public alongside the [[Ishikiri Maru]], a sword attributed to the [[Sanjō school]], during the shrine’s spring festival in April and autumn festival in October<ref name="刀剣ワールド">[https://www.touken-world.jp/religious-building/12991/ 石切劔箭神社（大阪府東大阪市） - 刀剣ワールド]2019th year 11月17日閲覧</ref>. This version of Ko-Gitsune Maru, at approximately 53.8 cm long, is classified as a [[wakizashi]] and is believed to differ from the tachi passed down by the Fujiwara clan<ref name="刀剣ワールド" />. However, its shortened tang and preserved signature suggest it was originally longer<ref name="刀剣ワールド" />.
Another sword named Ko-Gitsune Maru is housed at [[Isonokami Jingu|Isonokami Shrine]]. As of September 2018, it has been designated a Tangible Cultural Property of [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]<ref name="石上神宮">[http://www.isonokami.jp/about/c4.html ご由緒【宝物】 - 石上神宮]2019th year 11月17日閲覧</ref><ref name="奈良県">[http://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/143530/kenshiitiran.pdf 奈良県指定文化財一覧 - 奈良県（PDF版）]2019th year 11月17日閲覧</ref>. According to its legend, it was stolen during the late Edo period from the [[Emperor Suinin Mausoleum]] by grave robbers and sold to [[:en:Takatsukasa Masamichi|Takatsukasa Masamichi]], who, upon discovering its origin, donated it to the shrine{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}. The blade bears the signature of “Yoshinori,” attributed to the [[Ko-Bizen school]]{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}.
== Related Works == === Noh Play "Ko-Kaji" === {{能楽作品 |演目名=小鍛冶 |作者（年代）=不詳（室町時代） |形式= |能柄=五番目もの |現行上演流派= |異称= |シテ=稲荷明神 |登場人物=三条宗近、橘道成 |季節= |場所= |本説= |よみかな=こかし }} The play *[[Ko-Kaji]]* (*The Little Swordsmith*), written during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], recounts the tale of Sanjō Munechika and the divine assistance he receives from the god [[:en:Inari|Inari]]. While some characters, such as Tachibana Michinari, lack historical evidence, other details suggest connections to Emperor [[:en:Ichijō|Ichijō]]{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}.
==== Synopsis ==== The Emperor [[:en:Ichijō|Ichijō]] orders Tachibana Michinari to commission a protective sword. Michinari turns to Sanjō Munechika, renowned for his craft{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}. Struggling to forge a satisfactory blade, Munechika prays to his tutelary deity, [[:en:Inari|Inari]], and encounters a mysterious child who promises help. The child later reveals themselves as the deity Inari, assisting Munechika in crafting a divine blade. The finished sword, of exceptional quality, is named Ko-Gitsune Maru{{sfn|小和田|2015|p=31}}.
== Notes == {{Reflist|group="Note"}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == *[[List of Japanese Swords]] *[[Aitsuchi Inari Shrine]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:小狐丸]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:小狐丸]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:小狐丸]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11462866]] Wikidata name: kogitsunemaru [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11462866]] Wikidata name: kogitsunemaru [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Blacksmith Munechika, helped by a fox spirit, forging the blade Ko-Gitsune Maru, by Ogata Gekkō.jpg|thumb|200px|Illustration: *Inari Mountain Swordsmith* by [[:en:Ogata Gekkō|Ogata Gekkō]], based on the Noh play *[[Ko-Kaji]]*.]]
'''Ko-Gitsune Maru''' (小狐丸) is a legendary [[Japanese sword]] (*[[tachi]]*) said to have been forged during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. While multiple swords share this name, this article focuses on the blade attributed to the famed swordsmith [[Sanjō Munechika]].
== Overview == The sword is considered a masterpiece passed down within the [[Fujiwara clan]], with strong support for the theory that it was created by [[Sanjō Munechika]]<ref>{{Citation|和書|last=福永|first=酔剣|title=日本刀大百科事典|volume=2|publisher=[[雄山閣出版]]|date=1993-11-20|isbn=4639012020|ncid=BN10133913|page=234}}</ref>. By the late Heian period, the blade was in the possession of the [[:en:Kujō family|Kujō family]], later passing to the [[:en:Takatsukasa family|Takatsukasa family]] during the late [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], before eventually being lost{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}.
At [[Ishikiri Shrine]] in [[:en:Higashiōsaka, Osaka|Higashiōsaka, Osaka]], a sword said to be the work of Munechika is enshrined. It is displayed to the public alongside the [[Ishikiri Maru]], a sword attributed to the [[Sanjō school]], during the shrine’s spring festival in April and autumn festival in October<ref name="刀剣ワールド">[https://www.touken-world.jp/religious-building/12991/ 石切劔箭神社（大阪府東大阪市） - 刀剣ワールド]2019th year 11月17日閲覧</ref>. This version of Ko-Gitsune Maru, at approximately 53.8 cm long, is classified as a [[wakizashi]] and is believed to differ from the tachi passed down by the Fujiwara clan<ref name="刀剣ワールド" />. However, its shortened tang and preserved signature suggest it was originally longer<ref name="刀剣ワールド" />.
Another sword named Ko-Gitsune Maru is housed at [[Isonokami Jingu|Isonokami Shrine]]. As of September 2018, it has been designated a Tangible Cultural Property of [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]<ref name="石上神宮">[http://www.isonokami.jp/about/c4.html ご由緒【宝物】 - 石上神宮]2019th year 11月17日閲覧</ref><ref name="奈良県">[http://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/143530/kenshiitiran.pdf 奈良県指定文化財一覧 - 奈良県（PDF版）]2019th year 11月17日閲覧</ref>. According to its legend, it was stolen during the late Edo period from the [[Emperor Suinin Mausoleum]] by grave robbers and sold to [[:en:Takatsukasa Masamichi|Takatsukasa Masamichi]], who, upon discovering its origin, donated it to the shrine{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}. The blade bears the signature of “Yoshinori,” attributed to the [[Ko-Bizen school]]{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}.
== Related Works == === Noh Play "Ko-Kaji" === {{能楽作品 |演目名=小鍛冶 |作者（年代）=不詳（室町時代） |形式= |能柄=五番目もの |現行上演流派= |異称= |シテ=稲荷明神 |登場人物=三条宗近、橘道成 |季節= |場所= |本説= |よみかな=こかし }} The play *[[Ko-Kaji]]* (*The Little Swordsmith*), written during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], recounts the tale of Sanjō Munechika and the divine assistance he receives from the god [[:en:Inari|Inari]]. While some characters, such as Tachibana Michinari, lack historical evidence, other details suggest connections to Emperor [[:en:Ichijō|Ichijō]]{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}.
==== Synopsis ==== The Emperor [[:en:Ichijō|Ichijō]] orders Tachibana Michinari to commission a protective sword. Michinari turns to Sanjō Munechika, renowned for his craft{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}. Struggling to forge a satisfactory blade, Munechika prays to his tutelary deity, [[:en:Inari|Inari]], and encounters a mysterious child who promises help. The child later reveals themselves as the deity Inari, assisting Munechika in crafting a divine blade. The finished sword, of exceptional quality, is named Ko-Gitsune Maru{{sfn|小和田|2015|p=31}}.
== Notes == {{Reflist|group="Note"}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == *[[List of Japanese Swords]] *[[Aitsuchi Inari Shrine]]

'''Ko-Gitsune Maru''' (小狐丸) is a legendary [[Japanese sword]] (*[[tachi]]*) said to have been forged during the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}. While multiple swords share this name, this article focuses on the blade attributed to the famed swordsmith [[Sanjō Munechika]].
== Overview == The sword is considered a masterpiece passed down within the [[Fujiwara clan]], with strong support for the theory that it was created by [[Sanjō Munechika]]<ref>{{Citation|和書|last=福永|first=酔剣|title=日本刀大百科事典|volume=2|publisher=[[雄山閣出版]]|date=1993-11-20|isbn=4639012020|ncid=BN10133913|page=234}}</ref>. By the late Heian period, the blade was in the possession of the {{ill|Kujō family|en|Kujō family|WD=Q1791350|lt=Kujō family}}, later passing to the {{ill|Takatsukasa family|en|Takatsukasa family|WD=Q846799|lt=Takatsukasa family}} during the late {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, before eventually being lost{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}.
At [[Ishikiri Shrine]] in [[:en:Higashiōsaka, Osaka|Higashiōsaka, Osaka]], a sword said to be the work of Munechika is enshrined. It is displayed to the public alongside the [[Ishikiri Maru]], a sword attributed to the [[Sanjō school]], during the shrine’s spring festival in April and autumn festival in October<ref name="刀剣ワールド">[https://www.touken-world.jp/religious-building/12991/ 石切劔箭神社（大阪府東大阪市） - 刀剣ワールド]2019th year 11月17日閲覧</ref>. This version of Ko-Gitsune Maru, at approximately 53.8 cm long, is classified as a [[wakizashi]] and is believed to differ from the tachi passed down by the Fujiwara clan<ref name="刀剣ワールド" />. However, its shortened tang and preserved signature suggest it was originally longer<ref name="刀剣ワールド" />.
Another sword named Ko-Gitsune Maru is housed at [[Isonokami Jingu|Isonokami Shrine]]. As of September 2018, it has been designated a Tangible Cultural Property of {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture}}<ref name="石上神宮">[http://www.isonokami.jp/about/c4.html ご由緒【宝物】 - 石上神宮]2019th year 11月17日閲覧</ref><ref name="奈良県">[http://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/143530/kenshiitiran.pdf 奈良県指定文化財一覧 - 奈良県（PDF版）]2019th year 11月17日閲覧</ref>. According to its legend, it was stolen during the late Edo period from the [[Emperor Suinin Mausoleum]] by grave robbers and sold to {{ill|Takatsukasa Masamichi|en|Takatsukasa Masamichi|WD=Q594626|lt=Takatsukasa Masamichi}}, who, upon discovering its origin, donated it to the shrine{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}. The blade bears the signature of “Yoshinori,” attributed to the [[Ko-Bizen school]]{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}.
== Related Works == === Noh Play "Ko-Kaji" === {{能楽作品 |演目名=小鍛冶 |作者（年代）=不詳（室町時代） |形式= |能柄=五番目もの |現行上演流派= |異称= |シテ=稲荷明神 |登場人物=三条宗近、橘道成 |季節= |場所= |本説= |よみかな=こかし }} The play *[[Ko-Kaji]]* (*The Little Swordsmith*), written during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, recounts the tale of Sanjō Munechika and the divine assistance he receives from the god {{ill|Inari|en|Inari|WD=Q226639|lt=Inari}}. While some characters, such as Tachibana Michinari, lack historical evidence, other details suggest connections to Emperor {{ill|Ichijō|en|Ichijō|WD=Q21483074|lt=Ichijō}}{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}.
==== Synopsis ==== The Emperor {{ill|Ichijō|en|Ichijō|WD=Q21483074|lt=Ichijō}} orders Tachibana Michinari to commission a protective sword. Michinari turns to Sanjō Munechika, renowned for his craft{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}. Struggling to forge a satisfactory blade, Munechika prays to his tutelary deity, {{ill|Inari|en|Inari|WD=Q226639|lt=Inari}}, and encounters a mysterious child who promises help. The child later reveals themselves as the deity Inari, assisting Munechika in crafting a divine blade. The finished sword, of exceptional quality, is named Ko-Gitsune Maru{{sfn|小和田|2015|p=31}}.
== Notes == {{Reflist|group="Note"}}
== References == {{Reflist}}

By the late Heian period, the blade was in the possession of the {{ill|Kujō family|en|Kujō family|WD=Q1791350|lt=Kujō family|1=Kujō family}}, later passing to the {{ill|Takatsukasa family|en|Takatsukasa family|WD=Q846799|lt=Takatsukasa family|1=Takatsukasa family}} during the late {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, before eventually being lost{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}.
At [[Ishikiri Shrine]] in [[:en:Higashiōsaka, Osaka|Higashiōsaka, Osaka]], a sword said to be the work of Munechika is enshrined. It is displayed to the public alongside the [[Ishikiri Maru]], a sword attributed to the [[Sanjō school]], during the shrine’s spring festival in April and autumn festival in October<ref name="刀剣ワールド">[https://www.touken-world.jp/religious-building/12991/ 石切劔箭神社（大阪府東大阪市） - 刀剣ワールド]2019th year 11月17日閲覧</ref>. This version of Ko-Gitsune Maru, at approximately 53.8 cm long, is classified as a [[wakizashi]] and is believed to differ from the tachi passed down by the Fujiwara clan<ref name="刀剣ワールド" />. However, its shortened tang and preserved signature suggest it was originally longer<ref name="刀剣ワールド" />.
Another sword named Ko-Gitsune Maru is housed at [[Isonokami Jingu|Isonokami Shrine]]. As of September 2018, it has been designated a Tangible Cultural Property of {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}<ref name="石上神宮">[http://www.isonokami.jp/about/c4.html ご由緒【宝物】 - 石上神宮]2019th year 11月17日閲覧</ref><ref name="奈良県">[http://www.pref.nara.jp/secure/143530/kenshiitiran.pdf 奈良県指定文化財一覧 - 奈良県（PDF版）]2019th year 11月17日閲覧</ref>. According to its legend, it was stolen during the late Edo period from the [[Emperor Suinin Mausoleum]] by grave robbers and sold to {{ill|Takatsukasa Masamichi|en|Takatsukasa Masamichi|WD=Q594626|lt=Takatsukasa Masamichi|1=Takatsukasa Masamichi}}, who, upon discovering its origin, donated it to the shrine{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}. The blade bears the signature of “Yoshinori,” attributed to the [[Ko-Bizen school]]{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}.
== Related Works == === Noh Play "Ko-Kaji" === {{能楽作品 |演目名=小鍛冶 |作者（年代）=不詳（室町時代） |形式= |能柄=五番目もの |現行上演流派= |異称= |シテ=稲荷明神 |登場人物=三条宗近、橘道成 |季節= |場所= |本説= |よみかな=こかし }} The play *[[Ko-Kaji]]* (*The Little Swordsmith*), written during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, recounts the tale of Sanjō Munechika and the divine assistance he receives from the god {{ill|Inari|en|Inari|WD=Q226639|lt=Inari|1=Inari}}. While some characters, such as Tachibana Michinari, lack historical evidence, other details suggest connections to Emperor {{ill|Ichijō|en|Ichijō|WD=Q21483074|lt=Ichijō|1=Ichijō}}{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}.
==== Synopsis ==== The Emperor {{ill|Ichijō|en|Ichijō|WD=Q21483074|lt=Ichijō|1=Ichijō}} orders Tachibana Michinari to commission a protective sword. Michinari turns to Sanjō Munechika, renowned for his craft{{sfn|かみゆ歴史編集部|2015|p=56-57}}. Struggling to forge a satisfactory blade, Munechika prays to his tutelary deity, {{ill|Inari|en|Inari|WD=Q226639|lt=Inari|1=Inari}}, and encounters a mysterious child who promises help. The child later reveals themselves as the deity Inari, assisting Munechika in crafting a divine blade. The finished sword, of exceptional quality, is named Ko-Gitsune Maru{{sfn|小和田|2015|p=31}}.
